planting.note: Scarify seeds by soaking in near-boiling water for 24 hours before sowing to break dormancy and improve germination rates.

directions: Soak seeds in hot water and allow them to cool overnight before sowing directly into prepared ground in spring after frost danger has passed. Plant seeds 1 inch deep in well-drained soil and keep evenly moist until germination occurs. Transplant seedlings to their permanent location while still small, as the taproot establishes quickly and resents disturbance. Allow generous spacing to accommodate the wide canopy this tree develops at maturity.

planting.note: Propagate by hardwood cuttings taken in late winter or by digging and transplanting rooted suckers in early spring before new growth begins.

directions: Take hardwood cuttings 6 to 8 inches long from dormant stems in late winter and root them in a sandy propagation medium with bottom heat. Alternatively, dig rooted suckers from established clumps in early spring and transplant directly to the desired location. Plant in full sun in well-drained, even poor or sandy soil, as rich conditions encourage excessive suckering at the expense of flowering. Install a root barrier if planting near walkways or structures, as this shrub spreads vigorously by underground stolons.

planting.note: Lightly scarify seeds with sandpaper or soak overnight in warm water before sowing to improve germination, though seeds will often sprout without treatment.

directions: Sow seeds in spring directly into their permanent location or into deep containers to accommodate early taproot development. Plant in full sun in dry to moderately moist, well-drained soil — this shrub thrives in poor, alkaline, or sandy conditions where most plants struggle. Once established it requires virtually no supplemental irrigation and tolerates wind, drought, and severe cold with ease. Use in mass plantings for erosion control, shelterbelts, or wildlife hedgerows across challenging exposed sites.

planting.note: Crack the hard stone carefully with a nutcracker to extract the inner seed before sowing, as the endocarp significantly delays germination if left intact.

directions: Remove the seed from its hard stone before sowing, then plant 1 inch deep in sandy, well-drained soil in a warm, sunny location once all frost risk has passed. This tree demands heat and full sun and will not tolerate waterlogged or heavy clay soils; raised beds or slopes with excellent drainage are ideal in marginal climates. Protect young trees from frost in zones 8 and 9 during their first few winters until the trunk thickens and cold hardiness improves. Fruit production begins within three to five years of transplanting and increases substantially as the tree matures.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System

planting.note: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 to 6 weeks before sowing, then plant in deep containers to avoid disturbing the taproot at transplanting time.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds in damp sand in the refrigerator for four to six weeks before sowing in spring into deep pots to encourage taproot development before transplanting. Move seedlings to their permanent site while still young and small, choosing a spot in full sun with rocky, sandy, or otherwise fast-draining soil. Once established this tree is exceptionally drought-tolerant and long-lived, requiring no supplemental irrigation in Mediterranean or dry climates. Plant at least one male and one female tree to ensure fruit production, as this species is dioecious.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System
University of California Agriculture and Natural Resources
